text: Welcome to another episode of This Is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Ahmed from
The New York Times. Hey Ahmed. Hey Adrian, thanks for having me. Now I read your newspaper but tell
us a little bit about what The New York Times does. Our mission is simple. We seek the truth
and help people understand the world through our independent journalism. Our engineering teams like
serve more than 9 million subscribers over different product offerings like games, cooking,
and news. And you've been really building a whole bunch of new digital experiences. Tell
us about some of the challenges that you've had. That's true. When we started, we started
with like few AWS accounts to host all of our architecture and applications and then we grew
out of them. So we need like better ways to like govern all of these accounts and be able
to scale to serve all our engineering teams. Great. So I see you've got Control Tower here.
Tell us a little bit about the environment that you've built for your developers. Sure.
So when we talk about Control Tower is responsible for all of our accounts. Like we are talking
like from one account to multiple accounts here. And these accounts are separate from our
centralized account, which we host our runtime and where engineering teams host their storage
and other needs. Right. So services are running in these accounts. Maybe you can describe what
these accounts are for. Sure. So these are the engineering teams account, which they host like more
of the needs for the infrastructure from things like RDS, S3, and other things. But like when we look here,
this is our centralized runtime environment. We host our EKS Kubernetes clusters. And this
is like our networking account where like our main centralized gateways. Right. So tell us a little
bit about some of the policies that you've applied in these individual accounts. That's a very good
question. So in Control Tower, we have like a lot of policies that allow us to standardize and secure
our AWS accounts. So example for this policies, like you can't have like public access for S3, but we also have different set of rules that applies to our own

runtime environment. So we have to connect through IAM, for example. So like when you have a service deployed into one of the Kubernetes cluster here, like through our network policies, like we only allow you to connect to a specific account. And we also like allow you to connect to specific AWS IAM roles.
So this not only provides standardization, but also control as well, right?
That's correct. So through all of this, we can govern all the accounts and make sure that we have everything trailed through CloudTrail and AWS config.
Great. So how do your developers get started?

So they started like early in the process in the shared platform by like vending the accounts for them. And then developers start to build their applications through CI CD pipelines and push the image to ECR in their accounts. And then through the same CI CD pipeline, they start to deploy to our Kubernetes clusters. And as you can tell here, like this is our centralized runtime environment. And we go from here connecting like through transit gateway back to the account. So they can connect back to all of the services that they need to use.
Right. So using transit gateway internally to route the traffic and access between compute and storage.

That's correct. So like this is like already vended originally from control tower by an automated pipeline. So when we vend accounts, all of this are already connected together. So like developers and engineers don't have to do anything because all of these accounts connected together and we have all of the governance to watch over the networking happening between all different accounts.
So of course, there's infrastructure as code standardization, repeatable deployment is a big benefit. What are some of the other benefits that you've seen with this architecture?

So what we have seen with this architecture like standardization, that's the first thing that we are aiming for. But we also like allow engineering team to focus on achieving their business goal and reduce like their deployment time from like days to minutes.
Fantastic. Thanks for sharing your architecture with me.
Thanks for having me.
And thank you for joining us on This is My Architecture.
